ingrid-solheim.name: Ingrid Solheim
ingrid-solheim.description: Former regional tour operator with 11 years in Bergen. The E39 whisperer. If your itinerary has three fjords in four days, Ingrid will restructure it.
ingrid-solheim.longDescription:
I spent eleven years putting tourists into rental cars in Bergen and watching them drive off with itineraries that were never going to work. Three fjords, four days — the math doesn't add up, and the ferry schedules don't care about your optimism.

At NorgeTravel, I take the itinerary you dreamed up on Google Maps and replace it with one that will actually happen. That means exact ferry times, real driving durations, AutoPASS explained from scratch, and a clear answer to the question: Sognefjord or Hardangerfjord first?

My own favourite route in Norway is the old Rv13 up to Vikafjellet. You won't find it on any highlights list, and the view from the top at 4 AM in June, with the sun sitting just above the plateau, makes me understand exactly why I do this job.

bjorn-haugen.name: Bjørn Haugen
bjorn-haugen.description: DNT-certified guide with 25+ years in the Arctic. Former search and rescue volunteer. Zero tolerance for underprepared travellers in polar conditions.
bjorn-haugen.longDescription:
I grew up in Tromsø, which means I grew up in the dark. Four months of polar night every year teaches you things about light, warmth, and preparation that you can't learn anywhere else.

For over twenty years I have guided expeditions in Nord-Norge and Svalbard, and volunteered with Arctic search and rescue. I know what goes wrong when people underestimate the scale, the cold, and the darkness. At NorgeTravel, I write about the Arctic so that doesn't happen to you.

I will tell you what the Northern Lights actually look like on a cloudy February night, and why you need to book the following night too. I will also tell you which stretch of the E6 is the most dangerous in winter, and why Svalbard without a licensed guide in polar bear territory is not an adventure. It is a liability.

The Arctic rewards the prepared. I will help you become that.

marte-asheim.name: Marte Åsheim
marte-asheim.description: DNT-certified guide with 18 years in Jotunheimen and Rondane. The Mountain Code keeper. She has turned people back at the trailhead and has no regrets.
marte-asheim.longDescription:
I grew up in Lom with Jotunheimen as my backyard. As a child, Galdhøpiggen was just the mountain we see from the window. As a guide, it is the summit where I have learned the most about weather, human limits, and the importance of turning back in time.

After 18 years as a DNT-certified mountain guide, I write for NorgeTravel because too many travellers arrive at the peaks unprepared. They have seen the picture on Instagram, but not read about the 1,100 metres of elevation gain or what a DNT Red grade actually means.

My guides are not written to impress. They are written to send you home again.

My favourite cabin is Memurubu. It is not the highest, not the hardest to reach. But the kitchen smells of reindeer stew and there is a fire pit outside with a view across Gjende. That is enough for me.

silje-nygard.name: Silje Nygård
silje-nygard.description: Culinary journalist with 9 years covering Norwegian food culture. She knows where the chefs eat after their shifts, not where the tourist board sends you.
silje-nygard.longDescription:
I have spent nine years writing about food in Trondheim, which is, contrary to what Oslo journalists believe, one of the best food cities in Norway. The fish market at Ravnkloa. The Bakklandet cafes that have been there since before Instagram. The brewing culture that has nothing to do with the craft beer trend.

At NorgeTravel, I write for the traveller who is tired of the same ten Instagram locations and wants to understand what a Norwegian city actually feels like at 8 AM on a Tuesday in October. That is when the real city is happening.

I will tell you to skip the tourist-facing restaurants at Bryggen in Bergen. I will also tell you exactly where to go instead, down to the specific table I prefer when it rains. In Bergen, that is most days.
achievements: Deep network in Oslo and Bergen food scene

lars-erik-nordvik.name: Lars Erik Nordvik
lars-erik-nordvik.description: Born and raised in Svolvær, Lofoten. Commercial fishing background. He writes about the Lofoten that existed before Instagram found it.
lars-erik-nordvik.longDescription:
I grew up in Svolvær with the smell of stockfish as a constant in my childhood. My father fished skrei in winter. The summers belonged to the tourists. The winters belonged to us.

Lofoten has over the past ten years become one of the world's most photographed travel destinations. That is both wonderful and problematic. Wonderful because the islands deserve the attention. Problematic because the image being spread is not of Lofoten as it actually is. It is of Lofoten as seen through a wide-angle camera in golden hour.

At NorgeTravel, I write about the other Lofoten: the skrei fishing in January, the rorbuer that smell of wood and salt, and the man on the dock in Henningsvær who can tell you more about the sea than any guide.

I also write about the logistics. The Bodø ferry is the only ferry in the world where I have seen people cry from seasickness and astonishment at the landscape at the same time.
